summary: The author achieved 10th place by modeling all 16 (agent, target) mouse pairs jointly using a custom ST-GCN + Transformer architecture. Key technical choices include computing 24-dimensional features per keypoint (coordinates, velocity, acceleration, and relative inter-mouse metrics), normalizing coordinates per lab, and resampling videos to 30 FPS. The model directly optimizes a hybrid loss combining Cross-Entropy and a differentiable Macro Soft F1 approximation, with a schedule that shifts weight toward F1 over training. Final predictions are generated by probability-averaging an ensemble of models trained with different keypoint sets, sequence lengths, and pairwise vs. non-pairwise architectures.
extensive_summary:
## Overview & Architecture
The solution tackles a multi-agent mouse tracking and behavior classification task by modeling all 16 (agent, target) pairs jointly. The core architecture combines Spatial-Temporal Graph Convolutional Networks (ST-GCN) with a Transformer. Input sequences are processed through four ST-GCN blocks with temporal pooling, followed by keypoint attention pooling to reduce dimensionality. Pairwise feature extraction concatenates agent, target, and their relative features for all 16 pairs, which are then compressed and passed through a Transformer equipped with Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE). A cross-pair attention mechanism allows the 16 pairs to attend to each other before a final classifier outputs per-frame behavior logits.

## Preprocessing & Feature Engineering
Raw video coordinates are normalized per lab using mean and standard deviation, and all videos are resampled to 30 FPS via linear interpolation, with the original FPS passed as an additional feature. The author engineered a rich 24-dimensional feature set per keypoint: 8 dimensions for absolute position, velocity (Δt=2), and acceleration, plus 16 dimensions capturing relative position, relative velocity, Euclidean distance, and approach velocity to the other three mice. Keypoint names across different labs are mapped to a unified schema (e.g., `head → nose`, `spine_1 → neck`).

## Training Strategy & Loss
The model is trained using a hybrid loss function: `Loss = α × CE + (1-α) × MacroSoftF1`. The Cross-Entropy term covers all target-action combinations per agent, while the MacroSoftF1 term provides a differentiable approximation of the competition metric, excluding background classes. A schedule gradually reduces α from 0.2 to 0 over 32 epochs, eventually optimizing purely for Macro Soft F1. Invalid logits are masked to -inf before softmax using a triplet mask derived from labeled behaviors.

## Validation & Ensembling
Validation uses a 4-fold StratifiedGroupKFold scheme grouped by `video_id` and stratified by `lab_id`, ensuring CalMS21 and CRIM13 labs remain in the training folds. The final submission trains on all available data without a validation split. The ensemble combines models trained with varying keypoint sets (4, 5, or 7 keypoints), sequence lengths (160-224), and architectures (pairwise vs. non-pairwise). Predictions are averaged across models, with test-time augmentation (TTA) applied via horizontal flip averaging.
competition_flow: Raw video coordinates are normalized and resampled to 30 FPS, then converted into 24-dimensional spatial-temporal features per keypoint; these feed into an ST-GCN + Transformer model trained with a hybrid CE/MacroSoftF1 loss, with final predictions generated by probability-averaging an ensemble of models with varying keypoints, sequence lengths, and architectures.
data_processing: Resample all videos to 30 FPS (linear interpolation)
Normalize (x, y) coordinates per lab using mean/std
Map disparate lab keypoint names to a unified schema
Apply affine transforms (rotation, scale, translation, shear, flips), mouse ID shuffling, CutMix (temporal segments), and keypoint dropout during training
Apply horizontal flip averaging for TTA
features_engineering: 24-dimensional features per keypoint: normalized x, y coordinates
Relative position, relative velocity, Euclidean distance, and approach velocity to the other three mice
Original FPS passed as an additional feature
Lab, FPS, and action embeddings concatenated to pairwise features
cv_strategy: 4-fold StratifiedGroupKFold grouped by video_id and stratified by lab_id, with CalMS21 and CRIM13 labs always in training folds; final submission trains on all data without a validation split.
ensembling: The final submission averages probabilities across an ensemble of models trained with different keypoint sets (4, 5, 7), sequence lengths (160-224), and architectures (pairwise vs. non-pairwise), with test-time augmentation applied via horizontal flip averaging.
insights: Jointly modeling all 16 (agent, target) pairs with cross-pair attention significantly improves performance over non-pairwise approaches.
Directly optimizing a differentiable Macro Soft F1 approximation with a gradual schedule yields better metric alignment than standard Cross-Entropy alone.
Per-lab coordinate normalization and unified keypoint mapping are critical for handling heterogeneous lab data.
critical_findings: Models trained with pairwise feature extraction and cross-pair attention achieve a notable gap between LB (~0.525) and CV (~0.510) compared to non-pairwise models (~0.500 both), indicating strong generalization gains from explicit pair interactions.
Gradually shifting the loss weight from Cross-Entropy to MacroSoftF1 over 32 epochs is crucial for aligning training with the competition metric.
what_did_not_work: Semi-supervised learning with MABe22 unlabeled data
Merging rare classes per lab in training (e.g., AdaptableSnail: avoid → escape)
